title: Borodinsky: Russian dark rye
subtitle: Coriander-scented, molasses-dark sourdough loaf
excerpt: The distinctive Russian bread of Moscow bakeries: a dense, moist, deeply flavoured dark rye loaf leavened with a rye sourdough and sweetened with treacle, scented with coriander seeds. Slices thin and keeps for days.
sourceNotes: Borodinsky is a Russian bread with a documented history from the early nineteenth century, linked to the battle of Borodino in 1812. Early recipes appear in Russian household manuals of the 1860s-1880s. The coriander-seed topping and molasses (treacle) sweetening are both characteristic of the original Moscow formula. The method here follows a scald-and-sour two-stage structure documented in Russian baking literature.

content.text: Borodinsky is not a weekend project to rush. It asks for an active
rye levain
that has been fed the day before, a
scald

prepared 12 hours ahead, and a baking window of around 5 hours from mixing to the oven. The result is a loaf that slices thin without crumbling, keeps moist for 4 or 5 days, and pairs with herring, butter, or hard cheese in the way the bread was always intended.

attrs.title: What you need before you start

content.text: An active rye sourdough starter (100% rye, fed 12 hours before mixing). Dark rye flour, the coarser the better. Treacle (not molasses, the word is used interchangeably in American recipes but UK treacle is the right product here). Ground coriander and whole coriander seeds for the topping. Red rye malt (optional but traditional, adds the mahogany colour).
Ingredients
The scald (12 hours ahead)
The main dough
Method
Make the scald (evening before)

Pour the boiling water over 80 g of dark rye flour, the treacle, and the ground coriander and stir until smooth. The mixture will look like a thick paste. Cover and leave at room temperature for at least 12 hours. This activates enzymes in the rye that break down starches to sugars, giving the loaf its characteristic mild sweetness and dark colour.
Mix the dough

The following day, combine the scald with the remaining 320 g of dark rye flour, the warm water, sourdough starter, remaining treacle, and salt. Stir thoroughly, rye dough does not knead like wheat dough. There is no gluten network to develop in the same way; stir until fully combined. The dough will be very sticky, more like a thick batter than a bread dough.
Bulk ferment, 3 hours

Cover the bowl and leave at 24 to 26°C for 3 hours. Rye ferments faster than wheat and at higher acidity; the dough is ready when it has risen by about a third and small bubbles are visible at the surface. It will not double.
Tin and proof, 1 hour

Grease a 700 g (or 900 g / 2 lb) loaf tin generously with oil. Scrape the dough into the tin with a wet spatula, smoothing the top to a flat surface. Scatter the crushed coriander seeds over the top and press them lightly in. Cover and proof for 1 hour at room temperature until the dough has domed slightly above the tin rim.
Bake

Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional with a water tray on the floor. Bake for 15 minutes, then reduce to 180°C and bake for a further 45 minutes. The loaf is done when an internal probe reads 96 to 98°C and the sides have pulled slightly away from the tin. Turn out and cool on a wire rack for at least 2 hours before cutting, rye crumb continues to set as it cools, and cutting too early makes it gummy. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more; an oven thermometer gives a reliable reading.
Troubleshooting
Gummy crumb when sliced: cut too early. Rye must cool for a minimum of 2 hours; overnight is better. The crumb firms as it cools.
Dough not rising: the levain may not be active enough. Feed it 12 hours before at a ratio of 1:2:2 (starter:flour:water) to ensure peak activity.
Too dense: all 100% rye loaves are dense compared to wheat bread. Some additional wheat flour (up to 20% of the total flour weight) opens the crumb without compromising the flavour.
